key_insight: The paper proposes a multi-level ontology architecture that separates material science content from compliance needs and adds a mechanistic explanation skeleton to make materials ontologies reusable and interoperable.
engineering_relevance: It offers a practical blueprint for building ontology-based digital product passport infrastructure that can support ceramics and other materials domains under modern EU regulatory and supply-chain data demands.
method: Define four abstraction levels: L0 bridges, L1 material-agnostic lab notebook, L2 material-class-specific, and L3 categorical reasoning.
Organize Level 2 with a seven-tier mechanistic explanation skeleton: symmetry, energy/DFT, thermo/CALPHAD, kinetics, microstructure, defect chemistry, and bonding.
Implement and validate the design as OCO v0.94 with modular ontology components, bridge mappings, and SHACL constraints.
evidence_signal: The reference implementation reports 5,196 classes, 167,348 OWL axioms, 1,674 properties, 829 cross-ontology bridges, 1,172 SHACL shapes, and 163 competency questions.
watch_item: The architecture is powerful but may be costly to maintain and extend because its modular mappings and mechanistic tiers require consistent curation across many ontology modules and application contexts.
paper_title: A Multi-Level Architecture for Reusable Materials Ontologies -- The OntoCrafter Ceramics Ontology (OCO) as Reference Implementation
